blocks.statement: I can derive v_esc from KE = |U|, compute Earth's escape velocity, and compare it to other bodies (Moon, Mars).

blocks.whatWeKnow: If 2026-XJ had been moving FASTER than the local escape velocity, it could have slipped past Earth (or the Sun) and never come back.
blocks.connection: Same energy framework as the impact yield — but instead of asking 'how much PE did the asteroid gain falling in,' we ask 'how much KE does it need to climb back out.'

blocks.markdown:
## Climbing out of the gravity well

Throw a ball up: it comes back. Throw it FAST enough and it never does. 'Fast enough' is **escape velocity** — and it falls straight out of energy conservation plus the universal PE from earlier this unit (U = −G·M·m/r).

**Setup:** object at the surface (distance r from the center), mass m, given some launch speed v. At infinity, we want the object to JUST barely have KE_∞ = 0 (any extra is wasted).

Conservation of total energy: KE_surface + U_surface = KE_∞ + U_∞

½·m·v_esc² + (−G·M·m/r) = 0 + 0

½·v_esc² = G·M/r → **v_esc = √(2·G·M/r)**

Mass m cancels. Escape velocity is a property of the BODY you're leaving, not the escapee — a feather and a battleship need the same launch speed.

terms.definition: The minimum speed at distance r from a body of mass M such that the object can reach infinity with KE ≥ 0: v_esc = √(2GM/r). It doesn't depend on the escaping object's mass — m cancels out. Earth's surface escape velocity: ≈ 11.2 km/s (≈ 25,000 mph). Moon's: ≈ 2.4 km/s.
terms.cognate: Sp. velocidad de escape · Pt. velocidade de escape · HC vitès chape

blocks.title: Why small worlds are airless
blocks.markdown: Small bodies (Moon, Pluto, asteroids) have LOW escape velocities. That's why they don't hold atmospheres — gas molecules at typical room temperatures easily exceed v_esc and drift away.

blocks.title: See escape vs. capture
blocks.markdown: Run the asteroid-trajectory simulation below: try slower and faster approach speeds and watch which trajectories swing past Earth and away — and which fall in. That's escape velocity drawn as a picture.

blocks.prompt: EARTH'S ESCAPE VELOCITY. Compute v_esc at Earth's surface.
blocks.answer: ≈ 11.2 km/s ≈ 25,000 mph. Any slower → eventually falls back to Earth.

blocks.prompt: MARS ESCAPE VELOCITY. Compute v_esc at Mars's surface. M_Mars = 6.4×10²³ kg, r_Mars = 3.39×10⁶ m.
blocks.equationHint: Same recipe as Earth: plug into √(2GM/r).

blocks.prompt: MOON ESCAPE VELOCITY. Compute v_esc at the Moon's surface. M_Moon = 7.35×10²² kg, r_Moon = 1.74×10⁶ m. (This explains why Apollo astronauts could leave the Moon with a small ascent stage.)
blocks.equationHint: √(2GM/r) again — expect a much smaller answer than Earth's 11.2 km/s.

blocks.prompt: Check your Mars answer, then complete the atmosphere frame.
blocks.frame: Earth holds an atmosphere because the speed of a typical air molecule at room temperature is ___ Earth's escape velocity. The Moon doesn't because its v_esc is ___.
